title: The age of Jackson
description: Test your knowledge of Jacksonian democracy and politics during Jackson's presidency.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did some Southerners call the Tariff of 1828 the "Tariff of Abominations"?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They believed it unfairly benefited Northern manufacturers at the expense of Southern farmers.
They thought higher tariffs unfairly benefited Southern elites by increasing cotton exports to Europe.
They thought the tariff hurt Northern merchants by reducing trade with Southern farmers.
They believed it was unconstitutional because trade policies were set by individual states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Nullification Crisis of 1832 test the authority of the federal government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: South Carolina tried to ignore a federal law it disagreed with.
South Carolina argued that states should be allowed vote to change unfair federal laws.
South Carolina declared that no federal laws had power within its borders.
South Carolina passed its own tariff law to replace a federal law.

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to John C. Calhoun, why did states have the right to nullify federal laws?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: States cannot make treaties with other nations.

choices.content: Because the federal government gets its power from the states
Because states were empowered to make treaties with other nations
Because the federal government cannot intervene in disputes between states
Because a Supreme Court ruling gave states the power to reject federal laws

assessment_items.question_content:
**What did the Force Bill of 1833 show about President Andrew Jackson’s view of federal power?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how Jackson responded when South Carolina tried to ignore the federal tariff law.

choices.content: He believed the executive branch had the right to carry out national laws in every state.
He believed each state had the right to decide whether to follow national laws.
He believed the national government should avoid interfering in state matters.
He believed the Supreme Court should settle disputes between states and the national government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was the Nullification Crisis resolved?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Congress lowered a disputed tariff, and South Carolina agreed to follow the new law.
The Supreme Court ruled that South Carolina’s refusal to pay import taxes was unconstitutional.
President Jackson sent the army to occupy South Carolina until it obeyed federal law.
South Carolina seceded from the Union to protest a tariff.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the practice of rewarding supporters with political appointments and government jobs?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Spoils system
Rhode Island System
American System
Civil service system

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the primary reason that the U.S. government pursue the policy of Indian Removal during Andrew Jackson’s presidency?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To open Native American lands in the Southeast to white settlers and farmers
To protect Native Americans from violent conflicts with settlers and the military
To encourage Native Americans to become citizens and participate in the U.S. political system
To punish Native nations that refused to convert to Christianity or change their way of life

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Indian Removal Act of 1830 change the relationship between Native nations and the U.S. government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gave the federal government more control over Native lands and policies.
It led to greater equality between Native nations and American citizens.
It forced Native nations to follow all federal laws and adopt American customs.
It allowed Native nations to make individual decisions about relocation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did President Andrew Jackson oppose the Bank of the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He believed it helped only wealthy people and was unconstitutional.
He thought it charged farmers unfairly high interest rates.
He believed a private banking system would increase the power of the federal government.
He thought it wasn’t doing enough to support new settlers in the American West.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What happened after President Jackson took government money out of the Bank of the United States in 1833?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how state banks might have behaved after suddenly receiving large new deposits from the federal government.

choices.content: State banks loaned out too much money, leading to economic problems.
Congress voted to give the Bank even more power and money.
The Supreme Court ruled that Jackson had to return the money to the Bank.
Most Americans lost trust in financial institutions and stopped using banks.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was a leading cause of the Panic of 1837?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Jefferson was not the President in 1837.

choices.content: President Jackson’s war on the Bank of the United States
President Jefferson’s Embargo Act of 1807 against Britain
President Polk’s acquisition of the silver-rich territory of Nevada
President Van Buren's proposal for the Independent Treasury System

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did President Martin Van Buren fail to win reelection in 1840?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He was blamed for the economic depression that followed the Panic of 1837.
He angered voters by vetoing the Indian Removal Act and opposing westward expansion.
He lost support after being accused of corruption and accepting bribes from bankers.
He divided his party by opposing Jackson’s efforts to weaken the national bank.
